title: Ghidra Cheat Sheet
subtitle: Comprehensive Ghidra reference with workflow suggestions for reverse engineering.

sections.title: Suggested Reverse Engineering Approach
sections.text:
1. Start broad: identify file type, architecture, imports, strings, and entry points before diving into code.

2. Rename aggressively: functions, variables, structs, enums, and globals become much easier to reason about once named.

3. Work top-down first: find main logic, argument parsing, input handlers, comparison gates, network/file routines, and crypto/decode routines.

4. Use decompiler and disassembly together: trust neither blindly. Cross-check suspicious logic, pointer math, sign extension, bitwise ops, switch tables, and stack variables.

5. Follow data flow, not just control flow: where does input come from, how is it transformed, and where is it validated or used.

6. Create types early when possible: structs, function signatures, arrays, pointers, and enums often unlock better decompilation.

7. Bookmark and comment every important decision point: validation functions, decoding loops, anti-debug logic, import wrappers, vtables, dispatchers, and config loaders.

8. Iterate: first pass identifies landmarks, second pass renames and types, third pass reconstructs behavior.
